title: Sustainable-city development in Malaysia
filename: 2025/Evaluation report on Sustainable-city development in Malaysia.pdf

sections.lessons_learned:
The following lessons are proposed for consideration:
Timing and quality of project planning and approval require adjustment to context
changes.
This project was affected by changes in design from conception through to implementation.
The situation in Melaka was dynamic and already active through a range of interventions
to improve integrated planning. The objectives of the project remained valid but the
context for implementation changed. The shifts in institutional priorities, changes in
personnel and advances in other projects required close coordination and adaptive
management.  The implementation mechanism through a series of contracts was limited
based on contract deliverables which constrained the implementors from taking a more
proactive and informed approach to achieving project outcomes.
Insufficient attention to monitoring data and analysis from inception hinders learning of
project contribution to objectives.
The lack of monitoring data and tracking of outcomes undermined the capacity of the
project throughout the whole implementation period.  The initial results framework was
reduced to an enumeration of activities and achievement of outputs – rather than also
incorporating data to identify if the project is effectively contributing to wider results.  The
reliance on contracts for monitoring and reporting does not mean that there is sufficient
capacity to capture and analyze the extent and quality of data required for a demonstration
project that is designed to encourage wider uptake. There were important lessons arising
from implementation such as the need for Bahasa instruction, the need for more
coordination between implementing partners to avoid implementation gaps, amongst
others. The monitoring system of the project was not designed to capitalize on the relevant
information available during implementation that could be used for future improved
practices as per the expected outcome.
Incorporating exit/sustainability strategies from early in project implementation can
assist in building ownerships and strengthening the potential for long term impact.
GEF funding is allocated for specific purposes identified in the respective calls. In the case
of GEF-6, the phase length, identical with the project duration, was five years. Where the
proposed objective (or objectives) of a project is (are) targeting changes in behavior of
individuals, organizations, or even the public (i.e. transformative change), or requires
negotiation of complex changes, a five-year phase is a very short period for achieving
results and the risk of “unfinished” implementation is therefore significant. Unfinished
projects usually come with significantly reduced benefits and may even generate higher
costs than they generate benefits, including damage to image and the validity of the
purpose they were implemented for.
This project generated a range of benefits, but it was unclear what agencies would continue
the training or would continue to promote the uptake of the work developed through the
project. Explicit dialogue on agency roles and responsibilities and budgets through a
56
sustainability strategy preparation could have resulted in a clearer pathway to
sustainability by the project closure.
57

sections.findings:
are likely to continue post project implementation phase. Project benefits related to
integrated urban planning and management are difficult to measure because they are to a
large extent duplicating what Malaysia has in place already in one form or another. As far
as municipal finance is concerned, the likelihood of uptake of proposed changes is minimal
for the lack of institutional fit. There is also currently no plan for sustaining the Smart Grid
capacity building modules despite existing demand.
The following factors (including legal, governance financial and exogenous) may jeopardize
sustainability of project outcomes:
The SGRF exists only in English language at end of project. It is necessary to note the SGRF
has been published in 2022 in a limited number of printed copies and has not been widely
circulated even among key stakeholders. In its current English form, the likelihood of it
being debated in parliament and subsequently adopted as a policy document is minimal.
Additionally, the SGRF also lacks a linked financial model/plan to support the scaling of
Smart Grid initiatives piloted in Melaka and replication to other cities or states. Hence, this
framework risks ending up unused because it is not accompanied by financial strategies
38
for implementation. The project has not come up with any new business models beyond
the ones that were and are currently used in Malaysia (Zero CAPEX; Solar Power Purchase
Agreements, SPPA) and Energy Efficiency (EE) Performance Contracts (EPCs). As a result, it
is not clear how the Smart Grid project activities can be scaled up to the rest of Melaka.
The Scale-up and Replication Plan (SRP) for the Smart Grid project which currently only
exists in English language may impede scale-up and replication to other locations and
states due to language barrier for the majority of Malaysian stakeholders. Further, the
evaluation found the SRP lacking necessary elements of a proper scale-up and replication
plan such as implementation plan, available project tools and guidelines, and investment
cost for it to be effectively utilized by others. As mentioned in the introduction page of the
SRP, it is a comprehensive assessment of scalability and replicability of the Smart Grid
solutions. As such it is not a scale-up and replication plan per se.
The demonstration project dashboard as observed during field visit is now operational but
access to the dashboard is limited to TNB highly secured location in Melaka and at
roadshows. The language used to operate the dashboard is only in English and as such
limits practical use to Malaysian end-users and other interested stakeholders.
The server used to support the SG demonstration project only has sufficient capacity for
integration of the components of the SG demonstration itself. To roll out the integration to
the rest of Melaka state, which is technically possible, a larger server would need to be
acquired (and maintained). Additionally, challenges stated in PIR June 2024 and
information provided from key informant interviews such as technological limitations for
real-time monitoring data from smart meters for residential users, cyber security threats,
as well as data and system requirements to reliably sustain operation for a further 20 years
could jeopardize sustainability of project benefits.
There is no sustainability plan in place to secure the continuous availability of the training
packages (SG training) developed during the project. In fact, one of the agencies involved
in the SG project explicitly asked the evaluator about the plan for sustaining the SG training
because this agency is interested in continuing the training for further Government staff.
Although the project design for capacity building was to use the training of trainer (ToT)
approach in order to ensure sustainability of knowledge gained and to build institutional
capacity, the project has not implemented the training on Smart Grids and Smart Cities in
this manner. The 8 Smart Grid Modules used the modular approach to selected groups of
participants over a duration of 2 years while the 6 Smart City Modules use the virtual
knowledge sharing and forum approach to a large group of 600 participants over a span of
2 days. The Smart Grid training is reported to have reached 725 participants and 45.5% of
these participants achieved competency certifications at the end of the training. The
outcome of the Smart City training has not been specifically reported but the overall
capacity building for this project has been reported as “gave impactful outcomes to all
participants and organizers”. However, key informants interviewed have little knowledge
about the capacity building that their colleagues have received since it already happened
a while ago. In addition, other key informants mentioned a need for continuous capacity
building because officers who received intervention are likely to transfer to different
department within 3 years. This information indicates institutional capacity has not been
sufficiently built during project implementation although individual capacity may have
been built.
39
More importantly, based on information provided by key informants, to ensure
sustainability of benefits generated by the project, there is a need to identify key ministries
that will champion the Smart Grid initiative of the project as well as other benefits
generated by the project. The lack of a documented exit or sustainability strategy at the
time of the evaluation, and the concerns of country stakeholders on the ownership of
continuing support beyond the project makes it unclear how the project benefits can be
sustainable.
Regarding the SG demonstration project, based on diverse interviews with concerned
parties, businesses and Government entities that have participated, binding agreements
have been signed with TNB to continue the participation and support for 21 years. This is
the only output that has created the conditions for sustainability.
3.7
Progress to Impact
The examination of impact of an intervention includes analyzing high-level results/ effects,
significance of the intervention to those involved, unintended effects, and transformational
change (holistic and enduring changes in systems or norms).
Overall findings:  Findings revealed that the project did not specify indicators to measure
impact, but indicators related to Global Environment Benefits (GEBs) was used to measure
two core project results. There is no clear evidence of policy/ institutional changes in the
assisted institutions nor was there any evidence of attitude change to embrace Smart Grid
solutions among those who received intervention. Replication was not implemented during
the project but a scale-up and replication plan that lacks clear scale-up and replication
processes was developed.
In this project, impact has been defined as higher competitiveness and livability of cities.
However, no related indicators have been specified to measure such impact. Even the
parent project document does not specify any indicators that would allow measuring
progress to impact.
The highest level of indicators in the project refer to Global Environment Benefits (GEBs)
such as direct energy savings substitution with renewable energy, lifetime GHG emission
reductions and indirect GHG emission reductions. These indicators are meant to measure
the two project results namely, promotion of an integrated approach to urban planning
management that is guided by evidence-based, multidimensional, and broadly inclusive
planning process that balances economic, social and environmental resource
considerations; and building awareness, institutional capacity, and promoting investment
in climate risks mitigation technologies through demonstration projects.
This is a very far stretch. The impact indicators selected are at a much higher level than the
project highest objectives because the causal chain that connects integrated urban
planning or raising awareness directly to GEB is much longer than that, for example, of an
investment project that directly focused on these benefits. Moreover, whether integrated
urban planning as described is in place cannot be measured by direct energy savings; and
whether awareness has been built, and institutional capacity cannot be measured by GHG
reductions. Any project theory of change that would be able to bridge this gap would be
based on a gigantic leap of faith.
It is probably not without reason that the calibration of the indicators in this project has
been very tightly linked to the planned SG demonstration activities. Ultimately GHG and
energy savings can be used to represent the combined GHG reductions and energy savings
40
of the SG demonstration activities. However, they are not appropriate representation of
integrated urban planning, increased awareness, institutional capacity and investment.
Given the SG demonstration project was only completed at project closure in 2024, there
were no replications to other locations in Melaka and other parts of Malaysia. However, a
Scale-up and Replication plan (SRP) for Smart Grid has been developed as one of the
deliverables under this project. The evaluation found that the SRP was developed with little
input from the demonstration project but with input drawn from literature analysis and
stakeholder engagement, thus underlining the apparent lack of direct use of the
demonstration project. The SRP also merely assumes the demonstration project is
successful for it to be repeated elsewhere as there were no assessments made of the
demonstration project to determine what works, what did not work and what could have
been done differently for future projects. As such, the plan did not identify possible
replication and scale-up interventions from the demonstration project but merely
discussed the results of the scalability and replicability analysis in Melaka and three other
identified cities, which included readiness to scale and replicate as well as technical and
non-technical challenges that need to be addressed before replication and scale up. As
pointed out in the sustainability section the SRP lacks a model to guide the processes for
replication and scaling to merit being considered as a full replication and scale-up plan. It
is merely a study about replication and scale-up of Smart Grid in Malaysia without any
reference to the demonstration project implemented under this child project.
There is no clear evidence of policy/ institutional changes in the assisted institutions. For
integrated urban planning, please refer to effectiveness and relevance sections. The
demonstration project was only completed at the time of final evaluation of the project. As
such, its purpose is to build awareness, to build capacity and to promote investment in
climate risk mitigation technologies could not be achieved during the project
implementation period.
3.8
Gender Mainstreaming
The evaluation examined the extent to which UNIDO’s interventions have contributed to
improved gender equality and whether gender related dimensions were sufficiently
considered in the project.
Overall findings:  The project has not provided evidence as to how men and women were
affected by the project interventions.  Additionally, the project design has not included
meaningful gender dimensions other than an aspired percentage of women’s involvement
in project activities. There was equal participation for the Smart City activities, but female
participation was low in technical activities of the Smart Grid track of this project.
Upon review of the project document, the evaluation found reported in the project
document during the project preparatory phase, a preliminary gender analysis of the
country context and the project had been conducted, based on which potential gender
dimensions of project outcomes and outputs, as well as potential entry points for gender
equality and women's empowerment (GEEW) were identified. Some gender-disaggregated
indicators a
sections.conclusions:
4.1
Conclusions
The GEF6 project on sustainable-city development in Malaysia is viewed positively by a
wide range of stakeholders, has carried out numerous activities, and established some
formal and informal partnerships, but progress towards outcomes seems to have been
hampered by a focus on implementation of discrete activities in lieu of a strategic focus on
achieving high-level goals. The conclusions on the project related to the UNIDO criteria of
progress to impact, project design, project performance relating to relevance, coherence,
effectiveness, efficiency, sustainability of benefits and impact, gender mainstreaming,
project implementation management, performance of partners as well as environmental
and social safeguards (ESS), disability and human rights are as in the below:
14   https://myicsc.malaysiasca.org/
50
1.   Progress to impact:   The project was able to contribute the global environment
benefits of GHG emission reduction solely by implementing the Smart Grid
demonstration project. However, there is no clear evidence of policy/ institutional
changes in the assisted institutions nor were there any evidence of attitude change
to embrace a Smart Grid solution among those who received intervention.
Replication was not implemented during the project and a scale-up and replication
plan that, however, lacks clear scale-up and replication processes was developed.
Progress to impact at project closure can only be enhanced by ensuring that the
results of the demonstration projects are scaled up and made more directly
accessible to all interested users and that training programs established are
continued by a dedicated institution.
2.   Project design:   The overall design is a solution-driven design  which is heavily tilted
towards supporting GEF6 programming directions while the logical framework lacks
clarity and appropriate indicators to properly measure project outcomes. The
timeline and location change made to the project design while in line with
contextual shifts and stakeholders’ need led to reduced project efficiency (net
benefit).
3.   Relevance:  The evaluation found that the project is strongly aligned with the
policies and priorities of the donor (GEF) and implementing agency (UNIDO). Further,
while the project is moderately aligned to the policies and priorities of the
Malaysian Government it is not fully meeting the needs of the Malaysian
stakeholders as well as not being fully sensitive and responsive to the Malaysian
context. This is reflected in the failure to understand in depth the urban planning
structure and practices in Malaysia and misinterpreted them as “fragmented”. As
such, relevance is tilted to donor and implementing agency priorities.
4.   Coherence:  The project lacks synergies and interlinkages within the project itself
although there are some interlinkages between the project and other interventions
in the state system. But there is not enough clear evidence of support interlinkages
in the national system. In addition, there were some evidence to suggest limited
coordination with GEF funded sustainable city related projects has taken place.
Interestingly, the lack of synergies and interlinkages within the project was a result
of  implementation strategy based on sub-contracting specific groups of outputs
which introduced silo thinking and/ or behavior during implementation.
5.   Effectiveness:   The project did not reach all its goals by the end of the project. But
what the project did achieve was to contribute to the development of ongoing smart
grid initiatives in Malaysia. In particular, the project key barriers to achievement of
goals are attributed to the poor project design and a project logframe that does not
capture project results adequately.
6.   Efficiency:  About three-quarters of project activities (by activity count) were
implemented in a timely manner while the pandemic and management related
factors delayed the implementation of the Smart Grid activities leading to a 30
month extension which entailed reduced value of the benefits derived
(acknowledging the social time preference discount rate) and thereby efficiency
losses for the project. Despite its delay, the demonstration project contributed to
an intended reduction in CO2 emissions.
51
7.   Sustainability of benefits:   The project benefits related to GHG emission reduction
and RE generation are likely to continue post-project. Project benefits related to
integrated urban planning and management are difficult to measure because they
partially duplicate of what Malaysia has in place already in one form or another. As
far as municipal finance is concerned, despite polite participation by some
Malaysian stakeholders, the likelihood of uptake of proposed changes is minimal
for lack of institutional fit, and it therefore also should not be further promoted by
UNIDO. There is currently no plan for sustaining the Smart Grid capacity building
modules despite existing demand.   The Scale-up and Replication Plan lacks
necessary scale-up and replication processes for it to be effectively utilized.
8.   Impact:  Findings revealed that the project did not specify indicators to measure
impact but indicators related to Global Environment Benefits (GEBs) were used to
measure two core project results. There is no clear evidence of policy/ institutional
changes in the assisted institutions nor was there any evidence of attitude change
to embrace Smart Grid solutions among those who received intervention.
Replication was not implemented during the project but a scale-up and replication
plan that lacks clear scale-up and replication processes was developed.
9.   Gender mainstreaming:  The project has not provided evidence as to how men and
women were affected by the project interventions.    Additionally, the project design
has not included meaningful gender dimensions other than an aspired percentage
of women’s involvement in project activities. There was equal participation for the
Smart City activities, but female participation was low in technical activities of the
Smart Grid track of this project.
10.   Project implementation management:  The project team made some decisions and
corrective actions based on results of the mid-term review but in general  there is
no clear evidence that the project effectively used monitoring data to inform
programming decisions.   Outcome and output data overwhelmingly focused on
activities and was at times incomplete. It cannot be assumed that national partners
have capacity for monitoring and evaluation as required in development projects.
11.   Performance of partners:   UNIDO as the implementing agency was compliant with
requirements but did not adequately implement the responsibilities required for
the implementation of Component 2.  Monitoring at outcome level was not
effectively carried out and changes were not properly documented in a change log
to ensure that the rationale for changes were tracked. Knowledge management was
insufficient as was linkage with the parent project to effectively capture and share
learning. UNIDO did not adequately orient the project team, particularly when
changes occurred.
The national counterparts in general have shown strong commitment and technical
skills, but were not able to achieve project results due to barriers created by the
implementing agency such as implementation fragmentation and a logframe that
does not measure results accurately.
GEF as a donor provided appropriate input and supervisory support to the project
and adequately reviewed operational milestones but missed opportunities to
strengthen the progress towards the stated design objectives. GEF could also have
encouraged greater engagement between the parent and child projects.
52
12.   Environmental and social safeguards (ESS), disability and human rights:   There is a
well-developed generic Environment and Social Management Plan (ESMP) for this
project that includes environmental and social risks and mitigation measure as well
as environmental and social sustainability monitoring that is complete with
indicators. The project reported for ESS as required in the PIR but evidence of
monitoring is lacking as the reporting consists of selected activities that do not
relate to monitoring. Additionally, there are no changes to the ESS during the whole
period of the implementation of the project, all of which signals a lack of
understanding of ESS.
Evaluation ratings
Overall, the project is rated as “moderately unsatisfactory”. Table 5 provides an overview
of the ratings by criteria that are of relevance to UNIDO, in addition to the OECD-DAC criteria
which are also contained.

MS
H
Overall Assessment
MU
These ratings differ substantially from the ratings given by the MTR team during the mid-
term evaluation in 2021. It is important to note that the MTR apparently placed emphasis
on activity-based performance and did not place as much attention on the extent of
achievement of outcomes and the level of contribution of the project to those outcomes.
The MTR team was constrained by the CovID-19 pandemic in not having had the opportunity
to go to the field and collect first-hand information in confidential interviews.
4.2
Recommendations and Management Response
This evaluation provides the following recommendations that relate to project design,
project implementation and monitoring and evaluation.

Recommendation 2 :  UNIDO and GEF
needs to ensure future designs of
“child projects” include improved
and
timely
consultation
with
Malaysian
stakeholders
so
interventions are better to adapted
to context. This should involve:
•
a more effective process for
strategic
communication
with
senior
Malaysian
stakeholders
in
design,
establishment and during
implementation.
•
Specific initiatives need to
be closely considered for the
national
context.
For
example,
“municipal
finance” was not appropriate
to the Malaysian governance
financing
structure
and
despite polite participation
in the credit rating exercise
by national stakeholders,
should
not
have
been
interpreted as agreement
with the approach.
•
Project
implementation
mechanisms need to include
more robust processes to
ensure
adaptive
management
during
implementation, with clear
strategic checks to ensure
alignment
with
changing
priorities and the efforts of
other development partners.
•
Important
implementation
partners also should be
guided by outcomes rather
than
outputs
in
their
contracts and given more
flexibility in developing pilot
approaches.
In
future
project
designs,
including
under the
GEF-8
programming, UNIDO will work
to
strengthen
strategic
communication
channels
during
the
design,
establishment,
and
implementation phases.
UNIDO will also seek to embed
more robust mechanisms for
adaptive
management
throughout implementation of
the
envisaged
GEF-8
“Decarbonization
and
sustainable cities for a net-zero
future in Malaysia” project (GEF
Project ID 11343). This includes
introducing strategic review
checkpoints
that
assess
alignment
with
evolving
national
priorities
and
coordination with the activities
of other development partners.
Furthermore, we support the
recommendation
to
shift
implementation arrangements
toward
outcome-based
contracting
where
feasible,
allowing
implementing
partners greater flexibility in
designing and executing pilot
initiatives. This approach is in
line with UNIDO’s emphasis on
results-based
management
and adaptive learning.
These
insights
will
be
integrated into the design and
operational planning of future
projects to ensure increased
relevance,
ownership,
and
impact
